title: The Shifting Drivers of Global Liquidity

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: This Staff Report examines how the sensitivity of global liquidity components (international loan and bond flows) to global factors varied between 2007 and the mid-2010s. The estimated sensitivity to U.S. monetary policy rose sharply after the global financial crisis, peaked around the 2013 'taper tantrum,' then reverted toward pre-crisis levels. The paper attributes these fluctuations primarily to the degree of convergence among advanced economy monetary policies. Additionally, the responsiveness of international bank lending to global risk conditions declined steadily post-crisis, driven by increases in lending shares of better-capitalized banking systems.
key_points: Sensitivity of international loan and bond flows to U.S. monetary policy was highest immediately after the GFC and peaked around the 2013 Fed 'taper tantrum'
Post-crisis sensitivity to U.S. monetary policy reverted toward pre-crisis levels over time
Main driver of fluctuations in sensitivity to U.S. monetary policy was the degree of convergence among advanced economy monetary policies
Responsiveness of international bank lending to global risk conditions declined steadily throughout the post-crisis period
Decline in bank lending sensitivity to global risk was driven by increases in lending shares of better-capitalized banking systems
Paper is based on data through approximately the mid-2010s; PDF only, not available in full HTML
why_it_matters: Understanding how global liquidity flows respond to monetary policy and risk conditions helps market participants anticipate transmission mechanisms during policy shifts. The finding that better-capitalized banking systems drove the decline in risk sensitivity has implications for financial stability assessments and the effectiveness of traditional monetary policy transmission.
macro_context: This research provides historical context for understanding how global liquidity dynamics evolved during and after the GFC, relevant to assessing transmission of current monetary policy changes across borders.
market_structure_context: Discusses structural changes in global liquidity provision, specifically how the composition of bank lending across differently capitalized banking systems affects the overall sensitivity of international capital flows to risk conditions.
